county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Claiborne County formed 1802 from Jefferson County. County seat is Port Gibson, spared during the Vicksburg Campaign as Grant reportedly called it 'too beautiful to burn.' Named for William C.C. Claiborne, first territorial governor of Mississippi.
county_metadata.research_tips: Claiborne County (formed 1802 from Jefferson) has Port Gibson as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1802 and chancery court records. Claiborne County is one of the oldest counties in Mississippi with well-preserved records. MDAH has extensive microfilm of Claiborne County deed books, chancery minutes, marriage records, and estate inventories. Port Gibson was famously spared by Grant during the Vicksburg Campaign (1863) as 'too beautiful to burn.' The county had an enormous enslaved population on antebellum plantations. Windsor Ruins and other plantation sites reflect this history. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Vicksburg sub-district cover Claiborne County. Church records from First Presbyterian Port Gibson (1807), St. Joseph Catholic, and Baptist congregations are at MDAH and local churches. FamilySearch has digitized Claiborne County chancery records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Claiborne County deed books from 1802; Chancery Clerk in Port Gibson maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Clarke County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Quitman. Located in southeast Mississippi near the Alabama border. Named for Joshua G. Clarke, first chancellor of Mississippi.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clarke County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has Quitman as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court probate records. MDAH has microfilm of Clarke County records including land, marriage, and court records. Clarke County is in southeast Mississippi near the Alabama border, and early settlers migrated from Alabama, Georgia, and the Carolinas. BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov) document Choctaw Cession land sales. The Choctaw maintained a presence in the area after removal -- NARA Record Group 75 holds records of Choctaw who remained. FamilySearch has digitized Clarke County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1830s-1840s document early families. The Mobile and Ohio Railroad brought settlers in the 1850s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Clarke County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Quitman maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Clay County formed 1871 from parts of Chickasaw, Lowndes, Monroe, and Oktibbeha counties. County seat is West Point. Located in the Black Prairie region of east Mississippi. Originally named Colfax County, renamed 1876 for Henry Clay.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clay County (formed 1871 from Chickasaw, Lowndes, Monroe, and Oktibbeha) has West Point as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1871. For pre-1871 records, check the parent counties: Lowndes (Columbus), Monroe (Aberdeen), and Oktibbeha (Starkville). MDAH has microfilm of Clay County records. The county is in the Black Prairie region, historically a cotton plantation area with a large enslaved population. BLM GLO land patents document Chickasaw Cession entries. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Columbus sub-district cover the area. FamilySearch has digitized Clay County clerk records. Waverly Plantation records at MDAH document enslaved families. The Illinois Central Railroad influenced settlement patterns in the 1870s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Clay County deed books from 1871; Chancery Clerk in West Point maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Coahoma County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832. County seat is Clarksdale. A Delta county on the Mississippi River, famous as a birthplace of the Delta blues. The name is Choctaw for 'red panther.'
county_metadata.research_tips: Coahoma County (formed 1836 from Chickasaw Cession) has Clarksdale as county seat and is a quintessential Mississippi Delta county. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1836 and chancery court records. Coahoma County's population was overwhelmingly African American through the early 20th century due to the plantation economy. MDAH holds plantation records, slave schedules, and estate inventories. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA are critical for post-Civil War research. The Delta blues tradition is deeply rooted here -- Clarksdale is the 'crossroads' of blues history. BLM GLO land patents document original Chickasaw Cession sales. Levee board records document families along the Mississippi River. The 1927 Great Flood displaced thousands -- Red Cross records at NARA are useful. FamilySearch and Ancestry have digitized Coahoma County clerk records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Coahoma County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Clarksdale maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Copiah County formed 1823 from the Choctaw Cession of 1820. County seat is Hazlehurst. Located in southwest Mississippi. Named from a Choctaw word meaning 'calling panther.' The Natchez Trace corridor influences the county's early settlement history.
county_metadata.research_tips: Copiah County (formed 1823 from the Choctaw Cession of 1820) has Hazlehurst as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1823 and chancery court probate records. MDAH has microfilm of Copiah County land records, marriage books, chancery minutes, and estate inventories. As an early Choctaw Cession county, BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov) document original land entries. Copiah County had a substantial enslaved population on cotton plantations. Freedmen's Bureau records for central Mississippi cover the county. The county is near the Natchez Trace, and early settlers came from Georgia, the Carolinas, and Virginia. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1820s onward are important. FamilySearch has digitized Copiah County chancery records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Copiah County deed books from 1823; Chancery Clerk in Hazlehurst maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: 1872 courthouse fire destroyed many early records; some deed books survived
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Covington County formed 1819 from Lawrence and Wayne counties. County seat is Collins. Burned county: 1872 courthouse fire destroyed many early records, though some deed books survived. Located in the Piney Woods of south-central Mississippi.
county_metadata.research_tips: Covington County (formed 1819 from Lawrence and Wayne) is a burned county due to the 1872 courthouse fire, though some deed books survived. County seat is Collins. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and reconstructed records. For the burned period, use BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), Lawrence County and Wayne County records for pre-1819 entries, and any surviving deed fragments at MDAH. Covington County is in the Piney Woods region of south-central Mississippi, historically a timber and small-farm economy rather than plantation agriculture. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. Church records from Baptist congregations (strongly represented in the Piney Woods) are important supplements. FamilySearch has some Covington County records. Tax rolls at MDAH help trace families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Covington County deed books from 1819; Chancery Clerk in Collins maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.survival_notes: Significant losses from 1872 courthouse fire destroyed many early records; reconstructed records and surviving fragments at Chancery Clerk and MDAH

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: DeSoto County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832. County seat is Hernando. Mississippi's northernmost county, directly south of Memphis, Tennessee. Named for Spanish explorer Hernando de Soto. Now the most populous county in Mississippi due to Memphis suburban growth.
county_metadata.research_tips: DeSoto County (formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832) has Hernando as county seat and is the northernmost Mississippi county, bordering Memphis, Tennessee. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1836 and chancery court records. DeSoto County was heavily impacted by Civil War operations due to its proximity to Memphis, but courthouse records largely survived. MDAH has microfilm of DeSoto County land and chancery records. BLM GLO land patents (glorecords.blm.gov) document Chickasaw Cession entries. Many DeSoto County families had connections to Shelby County, Tennessee -- check Memphis-area records as well. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Memphis sub-district extend into DeSoto County. The county had significant plantation agriculture and enslaved population. FamilySearch has digitized DeSoto County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the late 1830s are held locally.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of DeSoto County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Hernando maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Forrest County formed 1906 from Perry County. County seat is Hattiesburg, the largest city in south Mississippi. Named for Confederate General Nathan Bedford Forrest. Home to the University of Southern Mississippi.
county_metadata.research_tips: Forrest County (formed 1906 from Perry) has Hattiesburg as county seat and is the largest city in south Mississippi. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1906. For pre-1906 records, check Perry County (New Augusta). Hattiesburg grew as a railroad and timber center. MDAH has microfilm of Forrest County records. The McCain Library and Archives at the University of Southern Mississippi in Hattiesburg holds extensive local history collections, including the de Grummond collection and civil rights era records. Forrest County was significant in the civil rights movement -- voter registration records and NAACP files are at USM and MDAH. FamilySearch has digitized Forrest County clerk records. City directories for Hattiesburg from the early 1900s are useful for tracing urban families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Forrest County deed books from 1906; Chancery Clerk in Hattiesburg maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.repository: Forrest County Chancery Clerk, False / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records for Forrest County from 1906; FamilySearch has indexed many Mississippi county marriage records; state marriage registration began 1926 through Mississippi State Department of Health

source_profiles.source_name: Forrest County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Forrest County from 1910 through 1950; includes slave schedules 1850-1860 listing enslaved persons by owner; agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide economic data; county formed after 1890 census

source_profiles.source_name: Forrest County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Forrest County from the General Land Office; Mississippi is a public land state; original patent documents are searchable and downloadable; especially important for Forrest County where the Piney Woods attracted small-farm settlers who purchased federal land

source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson / NARA / Fold3

source_profiles.source_name: Forrest County Historic Newspapers -- MDAH / Chronicling America
source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historic newspapers for Forrest County (False) at MDAH and Chronicling America; legal notices, obituaries, estate advertisements, marriage announcements, and community news; Mississippi Digital Library also hosts some digitized newspapers

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Franklin County formed 1809 from Adams County. County seat is Meadville. Located in southwest Mississippi in the transition between river counties and Piney Woods. Named for Benjamin Franklin.
county_metadata.research_tips: Franklin County (formed 1809 from Adams) has Meadville as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1809 and chancery court records. Franklin County is one of the older Mississippi counties with generally well-preserved records. MDAH has microfilm of Franklin County land records, marriage books, and chancery minutes. BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov) document original land entries. The county is in the hilly Piney Woods transition zone between the river counties and pine belt. Enslaved persons records, including estate inventories, are at MDAH and the chancery clerk's office. Freedmen's Bureau records for southwest Mississippi cover Franklin County. Baptist church records from the early 1800s are important sources. FamilySearch has digitized Franklin County chancery records. Adams County (Natchez) holds pre-1809 records for the area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Franklin County deed books from 1809; Chancery Clerk in Meadville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Franklin County Deed Books -- Chancery Clerk / MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Franklin County Chancery Clerk, False / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Franklin County from 1809; MDAH holds microfilm; Chancery Clerk maintains originals; Spanish and early American period land grants in the Natchez District predate federal land office records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1809; original deed books at Chancery Clerk in False

source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Franklin County; Mississippi is a public land state; original patent documents searchable and downloadable at glorecords.blm.gov; document original land entries from the federal government

source_profiles.source_name: Franklin County Tax Rolls -- MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax rolls for Franklin County listing property owners, acreage, livestock, and enslaved persons (pre-1865); essential for tracking family landholding and wealth; MDAH holds microfilm of county tax rolls

source_profiles.source_name: Franklin County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / MSGenWeb
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / MSGenWeb / Local historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Franklin County churches, community cemeteries, and family burial grounds; Find A Grave has growing coverage; MSGenWeb volunteers have transcribed many Franklin County cemeteries; particularly valuable when civil records are incomplete or destroyed

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: George County formed 1910 from Greene and Jackson counties. County seat is Lucedale. Located in southeast Mississippi in the lower Piney Woods. Named for Georgia statesman James Z. George. One of the newer Mississippi counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: George County (formed 1910 from Greene and Jackson) has Lucedale as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1910. For pre-1910 records, check Greene County (Leakesville) and Jackson County (Pascagoula). George County is in the lower Piney Woods of southeast Mississippi, historically a timber and turpentine economy. MDAH has microfilm of George County records. The county was sparsely populated in the 19th century, and settler families came largely from the Carolinas and Georgia via Alabama. FamilySearch has some George County clerk records. Baptist church records are the primary denominational source for vital events. Tax rolls at MDAH document landholding families. The Pascagoula River watershed influenced settlement patterns.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of George County deed books from 1910; Chancery Clerk in Lucedale maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov
